frameworks.name: sequential sampling
frameworks.one_line: deliberation as accumulation of evidence drawn from memory

frameworks.name: memory-based value construction
frameworks.one_line: long-term memory retrieval supplies evidence for value decisions

frameworks.name: value-based decision making
frameworks.one_line: choice between equally appealing options as a paradox of delay

core_question: Why does deciding between two equally appealing options take time, and what role does memory play?
key_assumptions: value is not fully precomputed but partially constructed at choice time
hippocampal long-term memory can supply evidence for value comparison
deliberation time reflects an evidence-accumulation process
manipulations: choices between pairs of familiar food items varying in subjective appeal
matched vs mismatched value pairings to modulate deliberation difficulty
key_findings: deliberation time on familiar food choices tracks hippocampal activity in long-term-memory retrieval regions
value is partially constructed during deliberation rather than read out as a fixed quantity
this constructive memory process changes both behavior and brain responses
interpretations: memory functions as a supplier of evidence in value-based decisions
the apparent irrationality of slow deliberation between equal options is resolved by constructive memory sampling
